Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 2|16
Before : JUSTICE M.CHAMATH.B.S. MORAIS
JUSTICE ANNALINGAM PREMASHANKER
Counsel: Naomal Goonewardena, Attorney at Law, with
Aparna Kulathunga, Attorney at Law, Akshina
Liyanage, Attorney at Law for the APPELLANT
Manohara Jayasinghe, Deputy Solicitor General
for the RESPONDENT.
Written Submissions of the Appellant :- 04.08.2025,18.12.2025
Written Submissions of the Respondent :- 21.04.2025
Argued on :- 28.10.2025
ANNALINGAM PREMASHANKER, J.
A.
This is an appeal by Lanka Securities (Pvt) Ltd (Hereinafter
sometimes referred to as The Tax Payer/ Appellant) from
the determination of the Tax Appeals Commission dated
INTRODUCTION
JUDGEMENT
APPEAL
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 3|16
19.06.2024 made in TAC appeal no. TAC/IT/NEW/231/2023.
The Respondent is The Commissioner General of Inland
Revenue (Hereinafter sometimes referred to as The
Respondent/CGIR).
The Appellant being dissatisfied with the Determination of Tax
Appeals Commission (Hereinafter sometimes referred to as
TAC) requested for an appeal by a case stated for the opinion of
the Court of Appeal by their communication dated 18.07.2024.
In the case stated dated 18.09.2024 following question were
raised for the opinion of the Court of Appeal:-
Whether the decision of the Tax Appeals
Commission to disallow a sum of Rs.
8,832,143.80 from and out of the tax loss of Rs.
16,436,061.70 brought forward from the year of
assessment 2016/2017 is wholly erroneous and
invalid in law in view of the losses for
2016/2017 being final for they said year in the
absence of any assessment for such year?
REQUEST
QUESTION OF LAW
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 4|16
On 28th October 2025, both parties concluded the argument. It
is found that the Appellant has furnished written submissions
on 04.08.2025 and 18.12.2025. The Respondent has furnished
the written submissions on 21.04.2025.
B.
There was one question of law presented for the opinion of the
Court of Appeal, in this Appeal.
“Whether the decision of the Tax Appeals Commission
to disallow a sum of Rs. 8,832,143.80 from and out
of the tax loss of Rs. 16,436,061.70 brought forward
from the year of assessment 2016/2017 is wholly
erroneous and invalid in law in view of the losses for
2016/2017 being final for they said year in the
absence of any assessment for such year?”
The tax payer submits its return for the year of 2018/2019.
The Assessor did not accept the return and issued an
assessment. Having aggrieved by the said Assessment, the tax
ARGUMENT
QUESTION OF LAW
TAX RETURN AND ADMINISTRATIVE REVIEW
ANANLYSIS
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 5|16
payer made a request for administrative review to the CGIR.
CGIR by his determination dated 04.08.2023 determined the
appeal by reducing the assessment.
The tax payer having not satisfied with the Administrative
Review by the CGIR, made an appeal to the TAC. TAC by its
determination dated 19.06.2024, upheld the decision of the
Inland Revenue Department and dismissed the appeal.
Thereafter, the Appellant being dissatisfied with the said
determination of the TAC requested TAC by its communication
dated 18.07.2024 to have a case stated for the opinion of the
Court of Appeal.
C.
Respondent raise an objection which warrants a dismissal of
the appeal even without considering the merits. The appeal
relates to the assessment year 2018/2019 which is governed
by the 2017 Inland Revenue Act. The said Act contemplates an
appeal to the Tax Appeals Commission and a further appeal to
the Court of Appeal. Section 144 is very explicit that an
appeal from the Commission to the Court of Appeal must
be by way of a notice of appeal filed not later than 14 days
APPEAL TO TAC
PROCEDURE
OBJECTION
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 6|16
from the determination. The taxpayer has NOT filed such a
notice of appeal. The Respondent states that the appeal is not a
valid appeal with the law.
Section 144 of IRA No. 24 of 2017 is as follows:
144 (1) “Either party to a proceeding before the Tax
Appeals Commission who is dissatisfied with the
decision of the Tax Appeals Commission may,
within one month after being notified of the
decision, file a notice of appeal with the Court of
Appeal; and the party so appealing shall serve a
copy of the notice of appeal on the other party to
the proceeding before the Tax Appeals
Commission”
(2) “An appeal to the Court of Appeal shall not be
made unless an appeal request to the Tax
Appeals Commission has first been made, and—
(a) a decision has been received from the
Tax Appeals Commission;
or
SECTION 144 OF THE IRA
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 7|16
(b) ninety days have lapsed since the
request for appeal to the Tax Appeals
Commission was made and no response
to the request for appeal has been
received from the Tax Appeals
Commission”
(3) “An appeal from a decision of the Tax Appeals
Commission to the Court of Appeal shall be made
only on a question of law”
(4) “Where an appeal is made from a decision of the
Tax Appeals Commission, the Tax Appeals
Commission shall provide a written statement of
the decision, including a summary of the
evidence, the Commission’s finding of the facts,
and their conclusions on the points of law
involved”
As per the Section 144 (1), it is necessary for an appellant to
file a notice of appeal with the court of appeal within one
month after being notified of the determination with
notice to the opponent.
ONE MONTH
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 8|16
In this instance, notice of appeal dated 18.07.2024 was filed in
the court of appeal with the motion dated 18.07.2024 and a
copy was forwarded to CGIR with a covering letter of
18.07.2024. The determination of the TAC dated 19.06.2024
has notified to the appellant on 19.06.2024 and as per section
144(1), the said notice of appeal has been filed within one
month of the intimation. Therefore the objection raised by the
Respondent has no meaning. There is no requirement to file the
notice of appeal within 14 days of determination.
D.
It is noted that the Appellant did not receive any Notice of
Assessment for the year of assessment 2016/2017. As a result,
the process of making an assessment in relation to the return
of Income for the year of assessment 2016/2017 has not been
completed.
As per the tax liability adjustment provided in the letter of
intimation dated 19th December 2018 for the year of
assessment 2016/2017, there were carried forward business
losses claimable up to 35% of the total statutory income.
INCOMPLETE ACTION
RESULTANT POSITION
NO MEANING
FURTHER ANALYSIS
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 9|16
Furthermore, there was a taxable income and a tax payable for
the relevant year of assessment. Therefore, the Respondent’s
failure to issue a notice of assessment for the year of
assessment 2016/2017, has rendered the entire assessment
procedure defective, null and void. Accordingly, the carried
forward losses as claimed by the Appellant should be
permitted.
The aforesaid loss incurred during the year of assessment
2016/2017 which is Rs. 18,583,092 and carried forward to the
following year which is Rs. 14,499,809, are to be treated as
final, and the Appellant is entitled for the entire brought
forward loss of Rs. 16, 436,061 claimed in the self- assessment
return for the year of assessment 2018/2019.
Item five of the extraordinary gazette notification No.2064/83
dated 01.04.2018 issued in terms of Section 194 of the IRA No.
24 of 2017, is as follows;
“Where any provision of the Inland Revenue Act, No.10
of 2006 provides for the deduction of any loss in
ascertaining the assessable income of any person for
GAZETTE NOTIFICATION
ENTITLEMENT
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 10|16
any year of assessment, and any balance of such loss
as at March 31, 2018 shall be deemed to be the loss
incurred for the year of assessment commencing on or
after April 1, 2018 under the Inland Revenue Act,
No.24 of 2017 and be deductible in accordance with
the Inland Revenue Act, No. 24 of 2017”
According to the self- assessment return for the year of
assessment 2018/2019, the Appellant has claimed a brought
forward loss of Rs. 16,436,061.70 in accordance with item 5 of
the Extraordinary Gazette Notification No. 2064/53 dated 1st
April 2018 issued in terms of Section 194 of the Inland
Revenue Act, No. 24 of 2017.
The aforementioned Gazette Notification clearly states that if
there is any balance loss as at 31st March 2018, then it shall be
deemed to be the loss incurred for the year of assessment
2018/2019 and therefore, shall be deducted in accordance
with the Inland Revenue Act No. 24 of 2017.
The Senior Deputy Commissioner has stated the following with
regard to the above.
CALCULATIONS
DEDUCTION OF LOSS
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 11|16
“As per the IRD records a loss adjustment had been
done at the finalization of the Return for 16/17.
Accordingly, loss carried forward to 17/18 is Rs.
6,895,891.10 (as per the 16/17 records). Further,
according to the IRD records, loss carried forward
balance to 18/19 (as per the 17/18 records) is Rs.
8,832,143.80. Therefore, the brought forward loss
allowable in the year of assessment under RAR is Rs.
The appellant states that once the Assistant Commissioner has
issued a letter of intimation under section 163 (3) of the Inland
Revenue Act, he should formally communicate the assessment
to the taxpayer in the form of a Notice of Assessment as per
section 164 of the Inland Revenue Act. Thereafter, if the
Appellant is not satisfied with the Notice of Assessment, an
appeal against such Notice of Assessment can be made to the
Commissioner General of Inland Revenue within 30 days from
the date of the Notice of Assessment as per section 165 of the
said Act.
FOLLOW UP
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 12|16
The Counsel for the Respondent in the course of the
submissions acknowledged that the Notice of Assessment has
not been issued in the instant case but has contended that the
absence of a Notice of Assessment does not necessarily mean
that no assessment was made, asserting that an assessment
was completed by way of the letter of intimation dated 19th
December 2018.
The Supreme Court in the case of Fonterra Brands Lanka
(Pvt) v. Commissioner General of Inland Revenue (SC Appeal
No. 187/2014) has held that:
“….as assessment is not complete, nor binding, until it is notified to the taxpayer within the statutory period….”

“It is fundamental precept of a just legal system that a public authority must act not only within its statutory mandate but also with transparency and accountability. To allow the state to held taxpaying entities in a state of perpetual uncertainty….would be to subvert the purpose of the law.” (p11)
Accordingly, the Respondent’s contention that a complete
assessment existed in December 2018 is untenable in law.
RESPONDENT’S ASSERTION
FONTERRA JUDGMENT 1
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 13|16
The Supreme Court has also rejected the argument that a letter
of intimation could constitute an assessment.

"...I find that although styled as an "intimation, the said letter cannot by any measure be equated with a Notice of Assessment within the meaning of Section 134 of the Act. While it contains certain preliminary calculations, these workings cannot be framed as a final and binding determination of liability." (p 21)
"..the letter before us is no more than a preliminary communication-a step antecedent to assessment-and cannot be given the legal effect of a Notice of Assessment."
The Respondent's reliance on the letter of intimation dated
19th December 2018 is therefore wholly erroneous. The
Respondent's position is contradictory to Fonterra case, where
the Supreme Court held the following:

"These authorities read together, I hold that an 'assessment' is not complete until it is communicated to the taxpayer by a notice of assessment served within the time limit... A letter of intimation is not an assessment, nor is it an internal departmental calculation." (p21)
FONTERRA JUDGMENT 2
FONTERRA JUDGMENT 3
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 14|16
The section 135(5) of the IRA states the following:
"(5) Where the Assistant Commissioner has made an amended
or additional assessment under this section, he shall serve the
taxpayer with notice, in writing, of the amended assessment
specifying the following:-
a) the original assessment to which the amended assessment
relates;
b) the amount of tax assessed and the basis upon which the
amended or additional assessment has been made;
c) the amount assessed as penalty (if any) in respect of the
tax assessed;
d) the amount of late payment interest (if any) payable in
respect of the tax assessed;
e) the tax period to which the assessment relates;
f) the due date for payment of any tax, penalty, and interest
being a date that is not less than thirty days from the date
of service of the notice; and
g) the manner of objecting to the assessment
In this instance, the above actions were not followed. The
notice of assessment not served in time.
STEPS UNDER SECTION 135 (5) OF IRA
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 15|16
Section 164 of the Inland Revenue Act as follows:

“An Assessor or Assistant Commissioner shall give notice of assessment to each person and each partnership who or which has been assessed, stating the amount of income assessed and the amount of tax charged….”
The contention of the Respondent is that the section 164
makes it clear that a notice of assessment is required to be
given only when income tax is been charged. In fact, by its very
definition, a notice of assessment is a document by which the
taxpayer is informed of the amount of income assessed and
“the amount of tax charged”.
It is the contention of the Respondent that no taxes were
charged to the 2016/2017 assessment year. Even with the
revision of the losses from 16 Million Rupees (as claimed by the
taxpayer) to the 8 Million Rupees (as urged by the assessor),
the taxpayer was not liable to pay income tax for that
assessment year. So, there were no taxes to be charged.
Therefore, a situation of sending a notice of assessment in line
with Section 164 does not arise.
PURPOSE OF NOTICE OF ASSESSMENT
NO NEED OF NOTICE
Judgment CA Tax No. 123/2024 Page 16|16
For the reasons adumbrated above, the question of law is
answered in affirmative, and as follows:
Q.N.1. YES
E.
As analyzed above and as the question of law raised in the case stated are answered in affirmative, the determination of the TAC dated 19. 06. 2024 is set aside and the appeal is allowed.
The Appeal is allowed and relief granted. No cost is ordered.
The Registrar is directed to forward a copy of the judgment to the tax appeal commission.
On this 19th day of March 2026
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
M. C. B. S. MORAIS
I agree.
JUDGE OF THE COURT OF APPEAL
CONCLUSION
ANSWER TO THE QUESTION OF LAW
